properties.Name: Sevvostlag (Kolyma)
properties.field_17: Databases that no longer exist or are censored

properties.Name: Norillag
properties.Notes: Norilisk uprising of 1953 - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norilsk_uprising

properties.Name: Belbaltlag
properties.Notes: Also know as Karelia
properties.Source: 8 of the most EVIL Gulag camps of the USSR - Russia Beyond

properties.Name: Berlag
properties.Notes: Special MVD camp for political prisoners

properties.Name: Dalstroy
properties.Notes: Organization (based in Magadan) managing road construction and the mining of gold in the Russian Far East; "Main Directorate of Camps and Construction of the Far North."
properties.Camp Type: Administrative trust (managed multiple camps, not a single site)

properties.Name: Bogoslovlag
properties.Source: Bogoslovlag, 1940-1953 | Russia's Necropolis of Terror and the Gulag

properties.Name: Rechlag
properties.Notes: Known for the Vorkuta uprising: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vorkuta_uprising

properties.Name: Gorlag
properties.Notes: Merged back into Norillag in 1954

properties.Name: Minlag
properties.Notes: After 1954 it was reorganized into an ordinary Mineralny Corrective Labor Camp

properties.Name: Dubravlag
properties.Industry: Special camp for political prisoners, "with a large population of Soviet dissidents":https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dubravlag
properties.Notes: Served as a prison of the Federal Penitentiary Service until 2005

properties.field_1: Camps Important to The Gulag Archipelago
properties.Name: Ek?bast?z
properties.Notes: Solzhenitsyn served here

properties.Name: Kengir
properties.Notes: Kengir uprising in May-June 1954

properties.Name: Solovki
properties.Industry: Camp mainly for the socialist opponents of the new Bolshevik regime; place of exile for "socially undesirabes"
properties.Notes: "Grandfather" of the gulags

properties.Name: Perm-36
properties.Notes: After 1953 the magnitude of the forced labor colony decreased; also known as ITK-6

properties.Name: Sevzheldorlag
properties.Notes: Headquartered at Knyazhpogost (now Yemva), Komi ASSR; merged with North Pechora ITL in 1950 to form Pechora ITL

properties.Name: Ozerlag
properties.Industry: Railway construction (Baikal-Amur Mainline branch lines, Tayshet to Bratsk and Ust-Kut)
properties.Notes: MVD special camp (Osoblag No. 7) for political prisoners, headquartered near Tayshet, Irkutsk Oblast

properties.Name: Ukhtpechlag
properties.Industry: Oil and coal mining, plus construction and logging
properties.Notes: Headquartered at Chibyu (now Ukhta), Komi ASSR; dismantled in 1938 into Vorkutlag, Ukhtizhemlag, Sevzheldorlag, and Ustvymlag

properties.Name: Siblag
properties.Industry: Forestry, coal mining, and agriculture (diversified across western Siberia)
properties.Notes: Headquarters relocated repeatedly between Novosibirsk and Mariinsk (Kemerovo Oblast); one of the longest-lasting and most economically diverse Gulag complexes
properties.Source: Siblag (1929-1960) | Russia's Necropolis of Terror and the Gulag

properties.Name: Usollag
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Solikamsk; numerous "lagpunkts" (camp points) across the northern part of then-Molotov (now Perm Krai) Oblast

properties.Name: Vyatlag
properties.Notes: Headquartered at Lesnoy, Kirov Oblast; over 100,000 prisoners of 80 nationalities passed through 1938-1956; continued as an ordinary penal colony after the Gulag's dissolution

properties.Name: Ustvymlag
properties.Notes: Created from a detachment of Ukhtpechlag; headquartered at Ust-Vym, later moved to Vozhayol, Komi ASSR; remained a corrective labor camp until 1960

properties.Name: Svirlag
properties.Industry: Logging (timber supplier for Leningrad and its oblast)
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Lodeynoye Pole, Leningrad Oblast; an estimated 100,000 people were incarcerated there over its existence; included a lagpunkt at the historic Alexander-Svirsky Monastery

properties.Name: ALZhIR
properties.Industry: Forced labor camp for women (the "17th special female detachment"); primarily wives and family members of "traitors to the Motherland"
properties.Notes: Subcamp of Karlag near Akmol village, Kazakhstan; roughly 18,000 women passed through over its existence; today the site of the ALZhIR Memorial Museum
properties.Camp Type: Special camp (women's/family camp; subcamp of Karlag)

properties.Name: Salekhard�Igarka Railway (Construction No. 501/503)
properties.Industry: Railway construction across the Arctic ("Transpolar Mainline")
properties.Notes: Known as the "Dead Road"; Construction No. 501 worked east from Salekhard while No. 503 worked west from Igarka; an estimated 80,000-120,000 prisoners were involved; the project was abandoned after Stalin's death

properties.Name: Kraslag
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Kansk, Krasnoyarsk Krai, with administration later moved to Reshoty; prisoners also built the Kansk hydroelectric plant

properties.Name: Ivdellag
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Ivdel, Sverdlovsk Oblast; one of several similar logging camps (alongside Usollag, Vyatlag, and Kraslag) established in the late 1930s in the Urals and Siberia

properties.Name: Unzhlag
properties.Notes: Headquartered at the railway station of Sukhobezvodnoye, Gorky Oblast; described in Lev Kopelev's memoir "To Be Preserved Forever"

properties.Name: Temlag
properties.Industry: Logging, wood processing, and railroad construction
properties.Notes: Headquartered at Yavas, Mordovian ASSR; operated one of the USSR's main camps for "wives of traitors to the Motherland" starting in 1937; liquidated in 1948 and reorganized into Dubravlag (Special Camp No. 3)
properties.Camp Type: Labor camp (ran a women's/family detachment from 1937)

properties.Name: Aktyubinlag
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Aktyubinsk (now Aktobe), Kazakh SSR

properties.field_1: Camps Outside the USSR's Core Territory
properties.Name: Macikai POW and GULAG Camps
properties.Industry: Forced labor (varied by period: construction, agriculture, and other work assigned to POWs and prisoners)
properties.Notes: Near Macikai, Lithuania; opened by Nazi Germany in 1939 as a POW camp holding Polish, French, British, and other Allied prisoners; captured by the Soviets in 1945 and run as Soviet POW camp No. 184 (1945-1948) before being converted into a Gulag forced-labor camp (1945-1955) holding German, Lithuanian, and other prisoners; illustrates how the same physical camp could pass between different prison systems
properties.Source: Macikai POW and GULAG camps - Wikipedia
properties.Camp Type: POW camp (German, then Soviet) ? later Gulag labor camp

properties.Name: Angarlag
properties.Industry: Railway construction, then construction of the Bratsk hydroelectric station and city of Bratsk
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Bratsk, Irkutsk Oblast; initially under the Western Directorate of Baikal-Amur Mainline Construction and Camps, later resubordinated directly to GULAG

properties.Name: Likovlag (Construction Site No. 204)
properties.Notes: Located between the villages of Likovo and Izvarino, Kuntsevsky Raion, Moscow Oblast; built Vnukovo Airport and a residential building for the Soviet elite on the Frunzenskaya embankment in Moscow; construction overseen personally by Kliment Voroshilov; closed soon after Vnukovo's completion in late 1941

properties.Name: Sukhanovo Prison (Sukhanovka)
properties.Industry: None (investigation and interrogation facility, not a labor camp)
properties.Notes: Established by Lavrentiy Beria on the grounds of the former Yekaterininskaya Pustyn Monastery near Vidnoye, just south of Moscow; officially "Special Object No. 110"; used to hold and interrogate high-profile political prisoners, including former NKVD chief Nikolai Yezhov; roughly 35,000 people passed through between 1938 and 1952

properties.Name: Yeniseystroy
properties.Industry: Steel production and non-ferrous metallurgy (administered mining/industrial camps as a directorate, similar to Dalstroy)
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Krasnoyarsk under chief Major General Panyukov; formally controlled about 10 camps, though only a few (including the Tayozhny camp near Kansk and the Tuimsky camp in Khakassia) were ever fully operational; its prisoners built the "Sibstal" steelworks and reportedly a uranium-processing facility in Krasnoyarsk
properties.Source: The camps of the Krasnoyarsk region - Memorial Krasnoyarsk
properties.Camp Type: Administrative trust (managed multiple camps, not a single site)

properties.Name: YeniseyLag (1947-1953, gold-mining era)
properties.Industry: Gold mining support (logging/timber supply, transport, and brickworks for the "Yeniseyzoloto" gold trust)
properties.Notes: Centered at the settlement of Sovrudnik (now Severo-Yeniseysky), Krasnoyarsk Krai; functioned essentially as an appendage of the Yeniseyzoloto gold-mining trust; an estimated 10,000 prisoners passed through, of whom roughly 10-20% were political prisoners; the name "YeniseyLag" was reused for at least three distinct camp organizations between the 1930s and 1953
properties.Source: The camps of the Krasnoyarsk region - Memorial Krasnoyarsk

properties.Name: OTB-1 (Yeniseystroy special technology office)
properties.Industry: Scientific and engineering design work (geological prospecting and industrial planning for Yeniseystroy projects)
properties.Notes: Located in Krasnoyarsk on what is now Mayerchak Street; held imprisoned university graduates and doctors of science, including geologists who studied mineral and uranium deposits; after its closure, its facilities and some former prisoners' work fed into the Krasnoyarsk State University law faculty and the Siberian Scientific Research Institute of Non-Ferrous Metals
properties.Source: The camps of the Krasnoyarsk region - Memorial Krasnoyarsk

properties.Name: Kaluzhsky ITL (Kaluglag)
properties.Notes: Built and maintained the Moscow-Kiev highway through Kaluga Oblast, just southwest of Moscow

properties.Name: Khimkinsky OLP (Khimki camp site)
properties.Notes: A camp site ("otdelny lagerny punkt") in Khimki, immediately north of Moscow, supporting construction of a Glavpromstroy mechanical base; one of many small satellite camp sites that ringed the capital

properties.Name: Moscow State University Construction Camp ("Stroitelstvo-90"/"Stroitelstvo-560")
properties.Industry: Construction (the iconic Moscow State University main building and the chemistry and physics faculty buildings on the Lenin/Sparrow Hills)
properties.Notes: First organized in December 1947 as ITL "Construction Site 90," renamed "Construction Site 560" in March 1949, and closed in November 1952; at least 8,000 prisoners are estimated to have worked on the project over its 5-year construction (1948-1953); largely omitted from the university's official histories, which credit the work mainly to free labor
properties.Source: Topography of Terror (topos.memo.ru) - Construction of MSU

properties.Name: Izmailovo Airfield Camp
properties.Industry: Airfield construction (runway and infrastructure for the Izmailovo Aerodrome)
properties.Notes: Established in Izmailovo, eastern Moscow, in May 1943; notable for an unusually female-heavy workforce, e.g. 775 women versus 145 men in July 1943; later staffed mostly by underage ("maloletka") prisoners while finishing runway repairs in 1944

properties.Name: North Moscow Water Supply Station Camps (Markovsky ITL / Sevvodstroy)
properties.Industry: Construction of Moscow's northern water-supply infrastructure (pumping stations and pipelines near Dolgoprudny)
properties.Notes: Operated under the Chief Directorate of Railroad Construction Camps from November 1949; had subdivisions near the villages of Gribki, Zabolotye, and Lianozovo; held 7,094 prisoners in 1949
properties.Source: Topography of Terror (topos.memo.ru) - North Water Station Camps

properties.Name: Istra Air-Defense Missile Base Camp (ITL "YeShch")
properties.Industry: Construction of Cold War-era anti-aircraft missile bases for Moscow's air-defense ring
properties.Notes: Established January 15, 1952 near Istra, Moscow Oblast, under "Construction Directorate No. 565" (a predecessor of the later Spetsstroy military construction agency); held 4,595 prisoners as of April 1952; illustrates how Gulag-style forced-labor construction continued into the earliest Cold War military buildup, just before Stalin's death
properties.Source: Topography of Terror (topos.memo.ru) - Dedovsk/Novy Jerusalem Camps

properties.field_1: Camps Important to The Gulag Archipelago
properties.Name: Perm-35 (Vsesvyatskaya, VS-389/35)
properties.Industry: None under its later political-camp role; the site began in 1942 as a logging and hydroelectric-construction camp (a division of the Ponyshsky ITL) before being converted to a maximum-security political camp
properties.Notes: Located at the settlement of Vsesvyatskaya, Chusovoy District, Perm Krai; one corner of the "Perm Triangle" alongside Perm-36 and Perm-37; held many of the USSR's most prominent dissidents in its final years, including Natan Sharansky; became the final holding camp for the last remaining political prisoners before the last were released on 7 February 1992
properties.Source: Perm-36 - Wikipedia / GULAG: History of One Camp

properties.Name: Tushino Aircraft Factory Camps
properties.Industry: Aircraft factory and runway construction (Factory No. 82 of the People's Commissariat of the Aviation Industry, and its airfield)
properties.Notes: Located in Tushino, northwest Moscow; subordinate to the Khimki ITL; included a 1943-45 camp specifically for building the factory's runway, whose workforce ranged from a few dozen to several hundred women, teenagers, and men over the project's life

properties.Name: Elektrostal Munitions Factory Camps (ITL Spetsstroy)
properties.Industry: Munitions/ordnance factory construction and reconstruction (Factory No. 12)
properties.Notes: "ITL Spetsstroy" was organized April 5, 1946 with its administration in Moscow but its construction sites in Elektrostal, Moscow Oblast; a 1951 incident records prisoners being marched through the city's streets to work sites in groups of 500 or more, leading to a clash with local civilians during a religious holiday

properties.Name: Amurlag (1938-1941)
properties.Notes: Created from the dismantled Bamlag; headquartered in Svobodny, Amur Oblast; disestablished in 1941 with its subcamps resubordinated to Burlag and Svobodlag

properties.Name: Yermakovo, Krasnoyarsk Krai
properties.Industry: Railway construction (administrative center of Construction No. 503, part of the Salekhard-Igarka Railway)
properties.Notes: Built in 1949 near Igarka specifically to house and administer prisoners of Construction No. 503; abandoned by 1955 after the project's cancellation

properties.Name: Amurlag (1947-1953)
properties.Industry: Coal mining, gold mining, railroad construction, and lumber
properties.Notes: A second, smaller camp reusing the "Amurlag" name, headquartered in Svobodny, Amur Oblast, unrelated in administration to the earlier 1938-1941 camp of the same name

properties.Name: Akukan mine
properties.Notes: Located in the Akukan Ravine north of Lake Baikal, Buryatia, near the village of Kholodnaya; one of the earliest forced-labor mining operations of the Soviet camp system, predating the formal 1930 establishment of "Gulag" as an administrative term

properties.Name: Nordvik
properties.Notes: An Arctic settlement and harbor-port on the Khatanga Gulf (Laptev Sea), Krasnoyarsk Krai, established by the Chief Directorate of the Northern Sea Route; though early hopes for commercial oil were not realized, salt was extracted on a large scale by forced laborers; closed when Allied personnel arrived at Nordvik during World War II, after which salt extraction continued using deportees rather than prisoners

properties.Name: Peschanaya, Irkutsk Oblast
properties.Industry: Fishing, fish processing, and sawmill work
properties.Notes: Located on the western coast of Olkhon Island, Lake Baikal; established for those convicted of "hooliganism and larceny"; prisoners were released after Stalin's death and the fish factory was abandoned by the 1950s; the site is now known for its distinctive wind-sculpted "walking trees"

properties.Name: Yertsevo
properties.Notes: One of more than a dozen camp complexes shared between Arkhangelsk and Vologda Oblasts; managed from 1940 by the Kargopolsky ITL directorate; survivor accounts say prisoners assigned to forest-cutting brigades rarely lasted more than two years; described in Gustaw Herling-Grudzinski's memoir "A World Apart"; the site continues to operate today as the IK-28 maximum-security penal colony
properties.Camp Type: Labor camp; site continues to operate today as a Russian penal colony

properties.Name: Abez camp
properties.Industry: None (a dedicated holding site for inmates no longer fit for labor)
properties.Notes: Located in Abez, Komi Republic; a special camp specifically for disabled and invalid inmates transferred from other camps across the Gulag system; eventually subordinated to Minlag; notable prisoners who died there include art historian Nikolai Punin and Lithuanian general Jonas Juodisius; a memorial cemetery and museum were established at the site by the Memorial society in 1989
properties.Camp Type: Special camp for disabled prisoners ("invalid camp")

properties.Name: Serpantinka
properties.Industry: None (execution and pre-execution holding site only)
properties.Notes: Located in the Kolyma region, though the exact location was never officially found; used during the Great Terror to enforce death sentences

properties.Name: White Swan (prison)
properties.Industry: Originally a penal colony to hold political prisoners but eventually used also for common criminals; today a maximum-security penal facility
properties.Notes: Located in Solikamsk, Perm Krai; in 1999 it was reclassified as a high-security colony for prisoners serving life sentences and remains in operation today (as IK-2 / VK-240-2), illustrating how some Gulag-era camp infrastructure persisted long past the system's formal 1960 dissolution
properties.Camp Type: Labor camp; site continues to operate today as a Russian maximum-security prison

properties.Name: Indigirlag
properties.Industry: Gold, tin, and tungsten mining; road construction (extension of the Kolyma Highway toward Magadan); housing construction; a small uranium-mining subsection operated until 1952
properties.Notes: Headquartered at Ust-Nera, Yakutian ASSR; operated under Dalstroy's USVITL (Directorate of North-Eastern Corrective Labor Camps), later transferred to the Ministry of Justice and finally the Ministry of Internal Affairs; of roughly 14,000 prisoners, about 3,000 were held on political charges (Article 58); closed July 19, 1958

properties.Name: Borlag
properties.Industry: Mining of radioactive (uranium) ore at the Yermakovskoye mining administration
properties.Notes: Headquartered first in the city of Chita, later moved to the settlement of Sinelga, Chita Oblast (now Zabaikalsky Krai); unlike most Far Eastern camps it was subordinated directly to the Gulag's main administration rather than to Dalstroy; one of several short-lived camps established in the late Stalin era to supply ore for the Soviet atomic program

properties.Name: Sorocklag (Soroka-Obozersky ITL)
properties.Industry: Railway construction (Soroka-Murmansk, Soroka-Obozerskaya, Onega, and Khizavar lines)
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Belomorsk, Karelia; established May 1938; grew from 400 to 17,000 prisoners within its first three months, over 3,000 of them political; reached its recorded peak of more than 52,000 prisoners in 1941; described by a contemporary Gulag official as resembling "a Gypsy camp, forever moving and migrating" because of its constantly shifting railway-construction subdivisions

properties.Name: Dzhidlag (Dzhidinsky ITL)
properties.Industry: Tungsten and molybdenum mining and ore processing
properties.Notes: Headquartered at the Dzhida combine near what became the town of Zakamensk, Buryat-Mongol ASSR; organized in February 1941 and placed under the NKVD/MVD's Main Directorate of Camps of the Mining-Metallurgical Industry (GULGMP) from January 1941 until 1953; by 1934-1942 the combine had become the USSR's largest single supplier of tungsten and molybdenum, alloying metals used in Soviet tank armor production during World War II

properties.field_1: Camps Important to The Gulag Archipelago
properties.Name: Peschanlag (Special Camp No. 8)
properties.Industry: None directly tied to political detention; prisoners were used in city/industrial construction, including a brewery, housing, and social infrastructure in Karaganda
properties.Notes: Headquartered in Karaganda, Kazakh SSR; organized September 5, 1949 on the former site of POW Camp No. 99; absorbed all of Luglag's camp divisions and the Ekibastuz division of Steplag in November 1950; closed August 24, 1955; one of its inmates was the Soviet-Estonian artist �lo Sooster
properties.Camp Type: Special camp (political prisoners, part of the Karaganda "special camp" network alongside Steplag)

properties.Name: Luglag (Special Camp No. 9)
properties.Industry: None (a dedicated holding site for inmates no longer fit for labor)
properties.Notes: Headquartered at Dolinskoye, Karaganda Oblast, Kazakh SSR; organized October 10, 1949 specifically to consolidate disabled ("invalid") prisoners transferred from other special camps in the region, in order to reduce costs of running infirmaries at multiple sites; closed September 1, 1951 and reorganized into a single camp division ("Spassk") that was folded into Peschanlag; Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn described its main division, Spassk, as the "all-union invalid home," noting that despite housing the sick and disabled it still had an extensive list of assigned work
properties.Camp Type: Special camp for disabled prisoners ("invalid camp")
